🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Staigue Fort
*Country:* ireland
*Region:* kingdom of munster
*County:* County Kerry
*Type:* fortified house
*Period:* Iron Age/Early Medieval
*Coords:* 51.80527778, -10.01583333
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Staigue_stone_fort
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Staigue%20Fort/@51.775,-10.028,15z
*Main Image:* https://img.castlecore.uk/staigue-fort.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Staigue or Staig is a ruined stone ringfort eighteen kilometres west of Sneem, on the Iveragh peninsula in County Kerry, Ireland. The fort is thought to have been built during the late Iron Age, probably 100 BC, as a defensive stronghold for a local lord or king.

_Listing 1 of 111_

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Segedunum Roman Fort
*Country:* england
*Region:* northern england
*County:* Tyne and Wear
*Type:* castle
*Period:* Roman (2nd century)
*Coords:* 54.98791, -1.53231
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Segedunum
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Segedunum%20Roman%20Fort/@54.99,-1.5317,15z
*Main Image:* https://img.castlecore.uk/segedunum-roman-fort.jpg
*Gallery:* 4 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* add source - https://www.northeastmuseums.org.uk/segedunum

*Description:*
Segedunum was a Roman fort at modern-day Wallsend, North Tyneside in North East England. The fort lay at the eastern end of Hadrian's Wall near the banks of the River Tyne. It was in use for approximately 300 years from around 122 AD to almost 400. Today Segedunum is the most thoroughly excavated fort along Hadrian's Wall, and is operated as Segedunum Roman Fort, Baths and Museum. It forms part of the Hadrian's Wall UNESCO World Heritage Site.

_Listing 2 of 111_

listing.name: Segedunum Roman Fort
listing.desc: Segedunum was a Roman fort at modern-day Wallsend, North Tyneside in North East England. The fort lay at the eastern end of Hadrian's Wall near the banks of the River Tyne. It was in use for approximately 300 years from around 122 AD to almost 400. Today Segedunum is the most thoroughly excavated fort along Hadrian's Wall, and is operated as Segedunum Roman Fort, Baths and Museum. It forms part of the Hadrian's Wall UNESCO World Heritage Site.

*Name:* Lewes Castle
*Country:* england
*Region:* south east london
*County:* East Sussex
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 11th century
*Coords:* 50.8729, 0.0076
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lewes_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Lewes%20Castle/@50.8743,0.0072,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZM8tYSbLhVMUujcftjUUEye2CrGUARX8-QJXsxffkRyk8BTZhQHye1ZQz9OiWdTydGsSXqcPVLMtGbyR85sI9w_LUD7pbTVIwRXHI9DBndtb_L2jdJUTvmiISo3T3CPaY8NEEcr1oQsztCoUx4=s4800-w1080
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Lewes Castle is a medieval castle in the town of Lewes in East Sussex, England. Originally called Bray Castle, it occupies a commanding position guarding the gap in the South Downs cut by the River Ouse and occupied by the towns of Lewes and Cliffe. It stands on a man-made mount just to the north of the high street in Lewes, and is constructed from local limestone and flint blocks.

_Listing 3 of 111_

listing.desc: Lewes Castle is a medieval castle in the town of Lewes in East Sussex, England. Originally called Bray Castle, it occupies a commanding position guarding the gap in the South Downs cut by the River Ouse and occupied by the towns of Lewes and Cliffe. It stands on a man-made mount just to the north of the high street in Lewes, and is constructed from local limestone and flint blocks.

imageAttribution.description: Two towers of Lewes Castle, West Sussex, photographed from the west.

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Reculver Towers
*Country:* england
*Region:* south east london
*County:* Kent
*Type:* abbey
*Period:* 3rd century Roman / 12th century towers
*Coords:* 51.3796, 1.1995
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/St_Mary's_Church%2C_Reculver
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Reculver%20Towers/@51.3783,1.195,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZN8HAWAU7ifuv5_T03IuOC5R-6Zm6_xiQBR2Xvih1-S32t89RsbYfp00ZCKVypgX6zBXwqhUnhil6IlRDu_f5jKFHPitLrM1_e-N-c7Ub_NR1woUWZkDbJ3lpjESII7QhLl3m_hCAHcyluTqw=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
St Mary's Church, Reculver, was founded in the 7th century as either a minster or a monastery on the site of Regulbium, the Roman fort at Reculver, which was then at the north-eastern extremity of Kent in south-eastern England. In 669, the site of the fort was given for this purpose by King Ecgberht of Kent to a priest named Bassa, beginning a connection with Kentish kings that led to King Eadberht II of Kent being buried there in the 760s, and the church becoming very wealthy by the beginning of the 9th century. From the early 9th century to the 11th the church was treated as essentially a piece of property, with control passing between kings of Mercia, Wessex and England and the archbishops of Canterbury. Viking attacks may have extinguished the church's religious community in the 9th century, although an early 11th-century record indicates that the church was then in the hands of a dean accompanied by monks. By the time of Domesday Book, completed in 1086, St Mary's was serving as a parish church.

_Listing 6 of 111_

listing.name: Reculver Towers
listing.desc: St Mary's Church, Reculver, was founded in the 7th century as either a minster or a monastery on the site of Regulbium, the Roman fort at Reculver, which was then at the north-eastern extremity of Kent in south-eastern England. In 669, the site of the fort was given for this purpose by King Ecgberht of Kent to a priest named Bassa, beginning a connection with Kentish kings that led to King Eadberht II of Kent being buried there in the 760s, and the church becoming very wealthy by the beginning of the 9th century. From the early 9th century to the 11th the church was treated as essentially a piece of property, with control passing between kings of Mercia, Wessex and England and the archbishops of Canterbury. Viking attacks may have extinguished the church's religious community in the 9th century, although an early 11th-century record indicates that the church was then in the hands of a dean accompanied by monks. By the time of Domesday Book, completed in 1086, St Mary's was serving as a parish church.

imageAttribution.description: Detail of a watercolour of Reculver Church painted in 1755 by L Sullivan, from a position north-east of the church, between the churchyard wall and the perimeter of the Roman fort.

*Maps:* https://maps.app.goo.gl/6dgFNMBpu7hQKHCH9
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZNV1v1mrNykVsZwa500_3iAgFLAXtBguSz81_M22JOXJVD3bp4q1NW6pilLy_1jUBSwDHoIhAmJagt6ipXTxbUyfvvCjWj_Dr2nK_vpZtg8lZWhoC6-nnBn4Na0CRFgWSGu-3dA2IwJqfKC2PRzmYYO=s4800-w800
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* add source - https://www.thornburycastle.co.uk/

*Description:*
Thornbury Castle is a Tudor castle in the town of Thornbury, in Gloucestershire, England, erected next to the parish church of St Mary. Construction was begun in 1511 as a further residence for Edward Stafford, 3rd Duke of Buckingham (1478–1521), of Stafford Castle in Staffordshire. It is not a true military fortress but rather an early example of a Tudor country house, with minimal defensive attributes.

_Listing 8 of 111_

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Grimsthorpe Castle
*Country:* england
*Region:* the midlands
*County:* Lincolnshire
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 13th-18th century
*Coords:* 52.7923, -0.4523
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grimsthorpe_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Grimsthorpe%20Castle/@52.767,-0.446,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/places/ANXAkqFcLrIC8aAkdItNh0RENC7Q5NUpUdGchX7gSV6d6v09X0OoXhlXY_FvsoQ0u2DB4h6HfddhBh1RZc2siPgUcQj7Om-uUYNma9E=s4800-w800
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* add source - https://grimsthorpe.co.uk/

*Description:*
Grimsthorpe Castle is a country house in Lincolnshire, England 4 miles (6.4 km) north-west of Bourne on the A151. It lies within a 3,000 acre (12 km2) park of rolling pastures, lakes, and woodland landscaped by Capability Brown. While Grimsthorpe is not a castle in the strict sense of the word, its character is massive and martial – the towers and outlying pavilions recalling the bastions of a great fortress in classical dress. Grimsthorpe has been the home of the de Eresby family since 1516. The present occupant is the 28th Baroness Willoughby de Eresby, granddaughter of Nancy Astor, who died at Grimsthorpe in 1964.

_Listing 9 of 111_

*Description:*
Hampton Court Castle, also known as Hampton Court, is a castellated country house in the English county of Herefordshire. The house is in the parish of Hope under Dinmore 4 miles (6.4 km) south of Leominster and is a Grade I listed building, which is the highest category of architecture in the statutory protection scheme.

_Listing 10 of 111_
